page: Amazon Web Services m8gn.medium is a general-purpose, network and EBS-optimized server instance powered by an ARMv9-based AWS Graviton Neoverse-V2 processor. It features 1 dedicated physical core with 1 vCPU running at 2.8 GHz, supported by 64 KB L1d cache, 2 MB L2 cache, and 36 MB L3 cache. The instance is equipped with 4.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. It has no local storage, relying entirely on network-attached storage, and operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 3 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks reflect its single-core architecture. The instance achieves average single-core performance, with a Geekbench single-core score of 1746.0 and a stress-ng single-core CPU score of 1692.68. Single-threaded tasks like bzip3 decompression show strong results at 14.21 GB/sec, while gzip compression performs at an average rate of 30.11 GB/sec. However, multi-core and multi-threaded benchmarks are limited by the single-vCPU configuration, resulting in lower multi-core scores. Memory bandwidth is measured at 30.44 GB/sec for large-block read operations and 11.67 GB/sec for write operations.

The m8gn.medium offers a cost-effective entry point for ARM64 development and lightweight workloads due to its low resource footprint and dedicated CPU allocation. However, the lack of local storage and limited single-core capacity present clear tradeoffs for resource-intensive applications. It is best suited for lightweight general-purpose workloads, microservices, basic web serving, and software development or testing environments optimized for the ARM64 architecture.

It features 1 dedicated vCPU, 4.0 GB of memory, and a 3 Gbps baseline network bandwidth, operating on the Nitro hypervisor with no local storage. Benchmarks show average single-core performance, with a Geekbench single-core score of 1746.0 and strong single-threaded bzip3 decompression at 14.21 GB/sec. However, multi-core and memory bandwidth metrics are limited by the single-core architecture. This instance provides a cost-effective, low-resource profile for ARM64 development, lightweight microservices, and basic web serving.
